domain: AI Agent Identity, Authentication & Access Control

scoring_methodology.demand: 0-10: based on validated demand signals (HN discussions, CVEs, vendor publications, VC theses, RSAC themes)
scoring_methodology.competition: 0-10: 10 = nobody does this, 0 = saturated market. Based on web search for existing products/solutions
scoring_methodology.feasibility: 0-10: 10 = straightforward to build, 0 = requires breakthroughs. Based on technical complexity

ranked_gaps.title: MCP Server Security & Authentication Gateway
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Enterprise MCP security gateway that sits between agents and MCP servers. Enforces OAuth per the MCP spec, adds tool-level RBAC, request signing/verification, rate limiting per agent identity, DLP scanning of tool inputs/outputs, and full audit logging. Ships as a Docker container or Kubernetes operator with a web dashboard.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Enterprise security teams deploying MCP-based agent workflows (financial services, healthcare, tech companies). Platform teams at companies with 50+ MCP servers. CISOs worried about the next CVE-2025-6514.
ranked_gaps.why_now: CVE-2025-6514 compromised 437K+ environments. MCP adoption exploding but security is an afterthought. Kong, PointGuard, MintMCP, and Lunar.dev have launched gateways in Q1 2026 — market is forming NOW. First-mover advantage is narrowing but no clear winner in security-focused positioning.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium — proxy architecture is well-understood; MCP protocol is relatively simple; OAuth integration is standard. Main challenge is keeping up with MCP spec evolution.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $500M-1B by 2028. MCP gateway/security is a subset of the broader AI infrastructure security market ($15B+ by 2028). Pricing at $500-2000/month per deployment = large TAM with enterprise pricing.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Kong, PointGuard AI, MintMCP, Lunar.dev all launched MCP gateways in early 2026. However, most focus on gateway routing rather than deep security. Opportunity is in security-first positioning with DLP, anomaly detection, and compliance features baked in.

ranked_gaps.title: Developer-Friendly Agent Auth SDK/Framework
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Open-source SDK (Python + TypeScript) that gives developers agent auth in 5 lines of code. Handles OAuth flows for MCP servers, third-party API OAuth (on-behalf-of), delegation token minting/validation, secrets management, and token refresh. Opinionated defaults with escape hatches. Free tier with hosted token management service.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Individual developers and small teams building AI agents. Startups shipping agent products. The 'Auth0 moment' for agent developers who just want auth to work without becoming IAM experts.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Auth0 launched auth-for-genai OSS, Composio has AgentAuth, Stytch has Connected Apps, Better Auth has agent-auth-protocol. Market is fragmented with no clear winner. Developers are actively comparing options (multiple 'best agent auth 2026' articles). Window to become the default SDK is open.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium — core OAuth/token logic is well-understood; challenge is breadth of integrations (MCP, A2A, various OAuth providers) and developer experience polish.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $200M-500M by 2028. Developer tools market with freemium model. Comparable to Auth0 early trajectory ($25M ARR at Series B). Every agent needs auth = massive horizontal market.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Auth0/Okta (enterprise, complex), Composio (integration-focused, not pure auth), Stytch (B2B-focused), Better Auth (protocol-level). No one owns the 'simple agent auth SDK' position yet. OSS-first strategy could win developer mindshare.

ranked_gaps.title: Runtime Least-Privilege Enforcement for AI Agents
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Runtime authorization engine that dynamically scopes agent permissions per-task. Mints ephemeral tokens with only the permissions needed for the current action. Integrates with OPA for policy-as-code. Provides a decision API that agent frameworks call before each tool invocation. Includes a policy editor UI and pre-built policies for common patterns.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Security-conscious enterprises deploying autonomous agents. Platform teams building agent orchestration. Companies in regulated industries (finance, healthcare) where over-permissioned agents are compliance violations.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Ping Identity launched 'Identity for AI' (GA March 2026) with runtime enforcement. BeyondTrust has Endpoint Privilege Management for agents. AWS published Well-Architected guidance. The problem is validated; enterprise budgets are allocated. But no pure-play startup owns this yet.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium-hard — requires deep integration with agent frameworks, fast policy evaluation at runtime, and ephemeral token infrastructure. OPA/Cedar provide policy foundations.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $1B-2B by 2028. Subset of the broader IAM market ($20B+). Enterprise pricing ($10K-100K/year) with high retention.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Ping Identity (enterprise incumbent), BeyondTrust (PAM), Oso (app-level authz), Aembit (workload IAM). Large players entering but with bolt-on features, not purpose-built. Startup opportunity is in agent-native design with framework integrations.

ranked_gaps.title: Unified Agent Identity Lifecycle Platform (Create-to-Revoke)
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Single platform managing the full agent identity lifecycle: registration, credential issuance, policy assignment, runtime monitoring, access reviews, rotation, and revocation. Agent inventory dashboard showing all agents, their permissions, last activity, and risk score. Integrates with existing IdPs (Okta, Entra, Google).
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Enterprise IT and security teams with 100+ agents. Identity governance teams extending their programs to cover agents. CISOs reporting to boards on agent risk posture.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Strata Maverics and Aembit both launched agent identity platforms in Q1 2026. 40% of orgs cannot discover/register agents (Strata research). RSAC 2026 was dominated by agent identity. Market is forming with venture funding flowing. But Strata and Aembit are the incumbents to beat.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: hard — requires broad integrations (cloud providers, SaaS, on-prem), governance workflows, and enterprise-grade reliability. 6-12 month MVP.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $2B-5B by 2028. Agent identity lifecycle management is the 'Okta for agents' category. Enterprise pricing ($50K-500K/year).
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Strata Maverics (identity orchestration), Aembit (workload IAM for agents), Okta (adding agent features), Microsoft Agent 365 (May 2026 GA). Crowded at enterprise tier. Startup opportunity may be in mid-market or vertical-specific positioning.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent-to-Agent Delegation Chain Security
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Delegation chain management library and service. Issues attenuation-only tokens (Biscuit/Macaroon format) where each hop in a multi-agent chain can only narrow permissions. Provides chain verification, visualization, and revocation. SDK for agent frameworks + hosted verification service.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams building multi-agent systems (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph users). Enterprises deploying agent-to-agent workflows where trust must be maintained across delegation hops. Security teams auditing multi-agent actions.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Google DeepMind published 'Intelligent AI Delegation' paper. DelegateOS shipped Ed25519-signed delegation tokens. Okta/Auth0 launched Cross App Access (XAA). CSA published delegation chain security guidance. The theory is maturing into products. Academic foundations are solid (Biscuit, Macaroons). First production-ready implementation wins.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: hard — cryptographic token design, chain verification at scale, integration with multiple agent frameworks. Requires deep security expertise.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $300M-700M by 2028. Niche within agent security but critical for multi-agent deployments. Could be a feature of larger platforms or standalone.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: DelegateOS (early OSS), Okta XAA (enterprise bolt-on), Yubico/Delinea RDTs (hardware). No dominant standalone solution. Deep technical moat possible with novel token format.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Secret Zero-Trust Bootstrap Problem
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Attestation-based agent identity bootstrap service. Agents prove their identity through environmental attestation (TEE, cloud metadata, code signing) rather than pre-shared secrets. Issues initial SPIFFE-compatible identity without any 'secret zero'. Supports cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP attestation), container (Kubernetes pod identity), and bare-metal (TPM) environments.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Security teams deploying agents at scale who cannot manually provision each agent. DevOps teams automating agent deployment. Zero-trust architects eliminating hardcoded credentials from agent pipelines.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Multiple Show HN posts solving this (Agent Passport, AIP). AGNTCY Identity OSS project. SPIFFE/SPIRE proving the model for workloads — agents are next. TEE integration maturing (Red Hat + SPIRE in Q1 2026). The bootstrap problem is the weakest link in agent security chains.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: hard — requires deep knowledge of attestation mechanisms across cloud providers, TEEs, and container runtimes. Must integrate with SPIFFE ecosystem. Security-critical code requires expert review.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $200M-400M by 2028. Infrastructure-level product with platform pricing. Could be acquired by SPIFFE/SPIRE ecosystem or cloud providers.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: SPIFFE/SPIRE (workload-level, not agent-specific), Agent Passport (Show HN stage), AGNTCY Identity (early OSS). No production-ready solution specifically for AI agent bootstrap. High technical moat.

ranked_gaps.title: Shadow AI Agent Discovery & Inventory
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Agentless discovery engine that finds ALL AI agents across an enterprise: cloud deployments, SaaS-embedded agents, RPA bots, browser-based agents, custom code agents. Automatic classification, risk scoring, owner attribution. Continuous monitoring for new agents. Integration with CMDB and ITSM tools.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: CISOs and security teams at large enterprises with shadow AI sprawl. IT governance teams needing agent inventory for compliance. Risk management teams assessing agent attack surface.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Zenity has comprehensive agent discovery. Cisco Identity Intelligence added agent discovery. Astrix expanded to shadow agent detection. Microsoft Agent 365 coming May 2026. Market is forming fast with multiple funded players. Late entrant risk is high.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: hard — requires discovery across many surfaces (cloud APIs, network traffic, SaaS APIs, browser extensions, code repos). False positive management is critical. Enterprise integration requirements are broad.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $500M-1B by 2028. Part of the broader NHI management market. Enterprise pricing ($50K-200K/year).
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Zenity (funded, comprehensive), Cisco (massive distribution), Astrix (NHI specialist), Microsoft Agent 365 (bundled). This space is getting crowded FAST. Differentiation would need to be in accuracy, coverage breadth, or vertical focus.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Behavior Anomaly Detection & Response
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Agent-specific UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics) platform. Learns baseline agent behavior patterns, detects anomalies (scope expansion, unusual API patterns, data exfiltration signals), and triggers automated response (suspend, scope-down, alert, quarantine). Agent-native: understands agent reasoning chains, not just API calls.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: SOC teams at enterprises deploying autonomous agents. Security teams needing runtime threat detection for agents. Companies where agent compromise could cause financial or data loss.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Microsoft launched runtime threat protection for agents (Agent 365). Palo Alto Prisma AIRS 3.0 covers agent security. Cyata has Agentic SPM. Bessemer VC thesis identifies this as defining challenge. Major vendors are moving in — but with bolt-on features, not agent-native detection.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: very hard — requires ML-based behavioral analysis, low-latency detection, integration with diverse agent frameworks, and automated response orchestration. False positive management is critical for SOC adoption.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $1B-3B by 2028. Part of the broader AI security market. Enterprise pricing ($100K-500K/year).
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Microsoft (Agent 365), Palo Alto (Prisma AIRS), Zenity (agent monitoring), Cyata (Agentic SPM). Large security vendors dominating with platform plays. Startup opportunity is narrow — would need deep agent-native detection that incumbents lack.

ranked_gaps.title: Open-Source Agent Identity Standard/Protocol
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Comprehensive open protocol specification for agent identity covering: registration, authentication, delegation, federation, and audit. Reference implementation in Python + TypeScript. Compatibility with MCP and A2A. IETF draft submission. Community governance model.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: The entire agent ecosystem. Platform vendors needing interoperability. Enterprises wanting vendor-neutral agent identity. Standards bodies (IETF, CSA, OWASP) looking for mature proposals.
ranked_gaps.why_now: AIP protocol proposed at IETF. Better Auth agent-auth-protocol shipped. Alibaba open-agent-auth released. Microsoft agent-governance-toolkit open-sourced. Mastercard launched Verifiable Intent for agent commerce. Multiple competing proposals — the one that gets adoption wins. Standards window is NOW.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: very hard — standards work requires community building, political navigation, broad consensus. Technical implementation is secondary to adoption. 12-24 month timeline to meaningful adoption.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: Not directly monetizable as a standard, but the reference implementation / hosted service around it could be $100M-300M. The company that defines the standard captures the ecosystem.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: AIP (IETF track), Better Auth agent-auth-protocol (implementations exist), Alibaba open-agent-auth (enterprise backing), Mastercard Verifiable Intent (payments). Highly fragmented. Winning requires community, not just code.

ranked_gaps.title: SMB/Startup Agent Security (Affordable Tier)
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: All-in-one agent security platform for startups: agent identity, auth, secrets management, audit logging, and basic compliance. Free tier for up to 10 agents. $50-200/month paid tiers. Self-serve onboarding, no sales calls needed. Open-source core with hosted service.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Startups and SMBs building AI agents who cannot afford Okta/CyberArk enterprise pricing. Solo developers and small teams who need 'good enough' security without becoming IAM experts. YC-stage companies needing SOC2 readiness.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Enterprise tools are priced at $50K+/year. Open-source options (SPIFFE, Cerbos) require expertise. DeepSecure and small OSS projects are emerging but none have product-market fit. The startup agent security market is completely underserved.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium — can build incrementally starting with auth + secrets, then add compliance and monitoring. Technical foundations exist in OSS. Main challenge is breadth without depth.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $100M-300M by 2028. High volume, low ARPU. Could be an acquisition target for enterprise vendors wanting down-market coverage.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: DeepSecure (very early), Infisical (secrets only), Permit.io (authz only). No all-in-one affordable agent security platform exists. Clear whitespace for a PLG startup.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent-Aware API Gateway
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: API gateway extension or standalone product that differentiates agent traffic from human/service traffic. Per-agent rate limiting, delegation chain validation, cost attribution per agent, behavioral throttling (loop detection, spend velocity monitoring), and agent identity-aware routing.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Platform teams managing APIs consumed by agents. API product managers needing agent-specific governance. FinOps teams attributing API costs to specific agents.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Solo.io launched AgentGateway. Kong added MCP gateway features. Zuplo published token-based rate limiting for agents. The '$1.6M weekend' story (agent runaway costs) went viral. But existing gateways are bolting on agent features rather than building agent-native. Linux Foundation backed agent gateway initiative.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium-hard — requires deep understanding of API gateway internals and agent traffic patterns. Could build as a plugin for existing gateways (Kong, Envoy) rather than from scratch.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $400M-800M by 2028. Part of the $5B+ API management market. Agent-aware capabilities will become table stakes.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Kong (major incumbent adding features), Solo.io AgentGateway (purpose-built), Zuplo (AI-native), Gravitee (pivoting). Hard to compete with established gateway vendors. Best as a plugin/extension or niche standalone.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Compliance & Audit Layer (SOC2/HIPAA/GDPR)
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Compliance layer that maps agent actions to specific SOC2/HIPAA/GDPR controls. Generates audit-ready reports showing which agent performed what action, mapped to which compliance control. Real-time compliance violation alerts. Pre-built control mappings for common agent patterns. Integrates with existing GRC tools (Vanta, Drata).
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Compliance teams at healthcare, financial services, and SaaS companies deploying agents. Auditors needing agent-specific evidence. Companies going through SOC2 audits with agent deployments.
ranked_gaps.why_now: AgentComplianceLayer.com is forming. Existing GRC tools (Vanta, Drata, Delve) handle human actions but not agent-specific compliance. Regulatory pressure increasing. But demand is moderate — most companies haven't been audited on agent actions yet.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium — control mapping is labor-intensive but not technically novel. Agent action logging is the hard part. Integration with GRC platforms provides distribution.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $300M-600M by 2028. Subset of the $15B GRC market. Enterprise pricing ($20K-100K/year).
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: AgentComplianceLayer.com (very early), Zenity (partial), Vanta/Drata (could add agent features). First dedicated agent compliance tool could win before GRC incumbents bolt it on.

ranked_gaps.title: Cross-Platform Agent Identity Federation
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Agent identity federation service giving agents a single identity across AWS, Azure, GCP, SaaS platforms, and on-prem infrastructure. Cross-platform policy sync ensuring consistent permissions everywhere. Built on SPIFFE/OIDC standards with agent-specific extensions.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Multi-cloud enterprises with agents operating across platforms. DevOps teams tired of managing separate agent identities per cloud. Security teams needing unified agent visibility.
ranked_gaps.why_now: HashiCorp publishing about identity fabric. OIDC-A proposal for OpenID Connect for agents. Strata Maverics positioning as identity orchestration. Market is early but the multi-cloud agent problem is growing.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: very hard — requires deep integration with every cloud provider's IAM, SaaS platform APIs, and on-prem infrastructure. Breadth of integration is the moat and the challenge.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $500M-1B by 2028. Part of the multi-cloud identity market. Enterprise pricing.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Strata Maverics (identity orchestration), Teleport (access plane), HashiCorp (identity fabric vision). Hard to build as a startup — requires massive integration surface area.

ranked_gaps.title: Human-in-the-Loop Approval Workflows for Agent Actions
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Universal approval engine for agent actions. When an agent hits a sensitive operation (financial transaction, data deletion, access escalation), it triggers a human approval workflow. Multi-channel (Slack, Teams, email, mobile push). Pluggable into any agent framework. Timeout and escalation policies. Approval audit trail.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Enterprises deploying agents for financial operations, HR, or data management. Compliance teams requiring human oversight for high-risk agent actions. Agent platform builders needing approval primitives.
ranked_gaps.why_now: IAPP calling for human oversight. Regulatory push (EU AI Act). Current implementations are ad-hoc Slack bots. ConductorOne and Apono do access requests but not agent action approval. Clear gap between regulation and tooling.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium — approval workflow engines are well-understood. Challenge is agent framework integration and low-latency approval flow that doesn't break agent execution.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $150M-300M by 2028. Niche but critical for regulated deployments. Could be a feature of larger platforms.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: ConductorOne (access requests, not agent actions), Apono (JIT access). No dedicated agent action approval tool. Could be a feature rather than a product.

ranked_gaps.title: Cost Attribution & Budget Enforcement per Agent Identity
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Per-agent cost tracking and budget enforcement service. Tags every API call, compute usage, and third-party service consumption to a specific agent identity. Sets budgets per agent with alerts and automatic suspension. Dashboard showing cost trends, anomalies, and optimization suggestions.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: FinOps teams at companies with many agents consuming expensive APIs. Engineering managers needing cost accountability per agent. Companies that have experienced runaway agent costs.
ranked_gaps.why_now: The '$1.6M weekend' story showed agent cost blowups are real. Agent gateway vendors adding cost tracking. But no dedicated agent FinOps tool exists. Demand will grow as agent deployments scale.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium — cost aggregation and budgeting are well-understood. Challenge is intercepting costs across diverse sources (LLM APIs, cloud compute, SaaS APIs, tool calls).
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $100M-200M by 2028. Agent-specific FinOps. Could be part of broader agent management platform.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: No dedicated product. Agent gateways (Kong, Solo.io) adding cost features. Cloud FinOps tools (Kubecost, CloudHealth) don't understand agent identity. Clear whitespace but may be a feature not a product.

ranked_gaps.title: Multi-Tenant Agent Isolation & Boundaries
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Multi-tenant agent isolation layer for SaaS platforms. Tenant-scoped agent identities, cross-tenant access prevention, blast radius containment, and per-tenant audit logging. SDK for agent-as-a-service platforms.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: SaaS companies building agent-as-a-service platforms. B2B SaaS deploying agents per customer tenant. Platform companies where tenant isolation is a security requirement.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Agent-as-a-service platforms are emerging. Oso and WorkOS address app-level multi-tenancy but not agent-specific isolation. Demand will grow as more SaaS platforms embed agents.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: medium-hard — requires deep understanding of tenant isolation patterns and agent execution environments. Must be bullet-proof — a cross-tenant leak is a company-ending event.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $200M-400M by 2028. Niche within the B2B SaaS infrastructure market.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Oso (app-level authz), WorkOS (B2B SaaS identity). Neither is agent-specific. Likely becomes a feature of agent platforms rather than standalone product.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Credential Forensics & Incident Response
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Agent-specific incident response toolkit. When a credential is compromised: map the blast radius (what did this agent access, what other agents share credentials), automated credential rotation without breaking dependent agents, dependency graph visualization, and post-incident reporting.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Security operations teams responding to agent credential compromises. Incident response teams needing agent-specific playbooks. Companies after their first agent security incident.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Bessemer thesis includes agent IR. RSAC 2026 theme of 'nobody owns the defense.' No major public agent credential breach yet — but when it happens, this becomes critical overnight. Pre-positioning opportunity.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: hard — requires agent identity graph construction, dependency mapping across diverse systems, and automated rotation that doesn't break workflows. Deep integration with secrets managers and IdPs.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $150M-300M by 2028. IR tooling market. Could be part of broader agent security platform.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: Entro Security (partial NHI IR), GitGuardian (detection only). No agent-specific IR tool. Demand is event-driven — will spike after first major breach.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Identity for Edge/IoT/Embedded Deployments
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: Lightweight agent identity library for edge/IoT devices. Hardware-attested (TPM/TEE), offline-capable identity verification, sub-MB footprint. Supports intermittent connectivity with local caching and sync. C/Rust implementation for embedded environments.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Companies deploying AI agents on edge devices (autonomous vehicles, industrial IoT, smart buildings). Defense/aerospace with air-gapped agent deployments.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Edge AI is growing but agent identity for edge is theoretical. No specific demand signals found. SPIFFE/SPIRE has limited edge support. Market is 2-3 years away from urgency.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: very hard — hardware attestation, offline operation, extreme resource constraints. Requires embedded systems expertise rare in the identity space.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $50M-150M by 2028. Highly specialized niche. Defense/aerospace could be early adopters at premium pricing.
ranked_gaps.competition_notes: SPIFFE/SPIRE (limited edge). No competition but also no demand. Classic 'too early' market.

least_competitive_gaps: GAP-015: Edge/IoT Agent Identity (8 — nobody doing it)
